--- Page 1 ---
Linear Programming Approach to
Deceptive Path Planning Game with Goal Selection
Violetta Rostobaya1, Yue Guan2, James Berneburg1 and Daigo Shishika1
Abstract— In adversarial settings, a mobile agent may strate-
gically plan its motion to influence an opponent’s inference
about its intended goal. We study deceptive path planning
in a scenario where a mobile agent aims to reach a pri-
vately selected goal while an adversarial observer allocates
limited defensive resources based on the observed trajectory.
Unlike classical path-planning and goal-recognition approaches
that model observers as passive inference process, our game-
theoretic formulation models them as strategic decision-makers.
For the resulting dynamic asymmetric-information game, we
develop an efficient solution method that combines a linear
programming formulation with the Double Oracle algorithm.
To evaluate performance, we introduce metrics that quantify
both the risk and the effectiveness of deception and provide
illustrative numerical examples.
I. INTRODUCTION
Deception plays an important role in domains such as mili-
tary operations [1], security planning [2], and autonomous
systems operating in contested environments [3]. In this
paper, we study deception in the context of motion planning,
where a mobile agent seeks to reach a privately selected tar-
get while a surveilling opponent allocates limited defensive
resources to candidate goals as illustrated in Fig. 1.
This setting is closely related to goal recognition [4]–[6],
in which an observer infers a mobile agent’s intent from
its trajectory, typically assuming that the agent follows a
cost-minimizing plan. In deceptive path planning (DPP),
goal-recognition models are often used to specify how an
observer infers an agent’s intent, against which the mobile
agent optimizes its trajectory to reach its true goal while
reducing inference accuracy [7]–[10]. However, these works
often treat the steering of observer’s beliefs as the primary
objective without considering its effect on the observer’s
decision making. We address this gap by formulating these
deceiver-observer interactions as games with explicit models
of observer’s action.
Prior works on game-theoretic formulation of DPP con-
sidered different environments, information structures, and/or
equilibrium concepts. The initial work that introduced a game
with a resource-allocating observer considered a grid-world
environment with worst-case payoff [11]. This work was
later extended to general graph environments with zero-sum
Bayesian formulation [12], where the goal was randomly
We gratefully acknowledge the support of ARL grant ARL DCIST CRA
W911NF1720181 and ARL grant W911NF2520011.
1Violetta Rostobaya, James Berneburg and Daigo Shishika are with
College of Engineering and Computing at George Mason University, Emails:
{vrostoba,jbernebu,dshishik}@gmu.edu
2Yue Guan is with the School of Aerospace Engineering, Georgia
Institute of Technology, Email: yguan44@gatech.edu
Start
g1
g2
g3
True goal
Start
g1
g2
g3
True goal
Start
g1
g2
g3
The Attacker’s perspective
The Defender’s perspective
Fig. 1.
The Attacker aims to reach its selected goal g1. The Defender
does not know the true goal, but it aims to infer the Attacker’s intentions
and allocate maximum amount of defensive resources to the true goal.
assigned to the agent with a known prior probability. Both
works assumed that the deceiver must reach one of two
candidate goals (i.e., there are two possible types of agents).
In contrast, we generalize the DPP game to a multi-goal
scenario. Moreover, we allow the deceiver to select its goal
at the start of the game, thereby eliminating the need to
assume a prior probability distribution over goals.
Computational complexity is another important consider-
ation for the DPP game. Prior works have leveraged specific
geometric structure to induce tractable solutions [11], [12].
More generally, dynamic imperfect-information games such
as ours that allow game-tree representation may be solved us-
ing a linear programming (LP) [13] or bilinear program [14]
under certain structural assumptions. Specifically, zero-sum
asymmetric-information games can be solved with LP when
the state is controlled solely by the informed player [15].
However, representing the game in extensive form leads to a
game tree whose size grows exponentially with the planning
horizon, making direct solution methods computationally
challenging for large environments.
To address this computational challenge, we adapt the
Double Oracle algorithm developed for finite extensive-form
games (XDO) [16], which incrementally expands the game
tree until it is sufficient for an ε-Nash equilibrium. In
particular, we combine this approach with the LP formu-
lation [15], yielding an iterative method that is well-suited
for our multi-goal DPP game. Leveraging the one-sided
incomplete-information structure and a particular choice of
default strategies [16], [17], we are able to solve DPP game
with indefinite horizon (i.e., the termination time is not
specified apriori) and compute an exact Nash equilibrium
rather than an ε-Nash equilibrium.
The main contributions of this work are: (i) formulation
of a new variant of the DPP game with goal selection,
arXiv:2605.16548v1  [eess.SY]  15 May 2026


--- Page 2 ---
(ii) solution of the game via an algorithm that integrates
the LP formulation with the Double Oracle algorithm for
extensive-form games; and (iii) metrics to evaluate risk and
effectiveness of emergent deceptive behavior, benchmarked
against the complete-information setting.
II. PRELIMINARIES
We consider an asymmetric information deceptive path plan-
ning game between a mobile agent, Attacker, and an observ-
ing agent, Defender, as illustrated in Fig. 1. The Attacker
moves in a graph environment containing multiple candidate
goals. At the start of the game, the Attacker selects a goal
that it must reach. The Defender, lacking knowledge of the
selected goal, must infer it based solely on observations
of the Attacker’s trajectory. The Defender seeks to allocate
defense resources to the selected goal before the Attacker
arrives. This setting naturally incentivizes the Attacker to
move in a way that conceals its true goal and misleads the
Defender into allocating resources to the wrong goals.
Environment: The graph is denoted as G = ⟨V, E, w⟩,
where V is a finite set of nodes, and E ⊆V × V is the
set of edges. We use N(s) to represent the out-neighbors of
s on G. The positive edge weight is given by the function
w : E →R>0. The weight w(s, s′) represents the amount
of defense resource the Defender can allocate to one of the
goals while the Attacker is traversing through edge (s, s′).
The set of candidate goals is denoted by Θ ⊆V. At the
beginning of the game, the Attacker selects a goal θ ∈Θ.
An Attacker that selects goal θ is said to be of type θ. Once
the goal is selected, the Attacker must commit to it for the
remainder of the game; that is, the Attacker’s type remains
fixed.
Dynamics: The game state at time t is given by the
Attacker’s position st ∈V. At t = −1, the Attacker makes a
pre-game decision and selects its type from the set Θ. This
choice is not revealed to the Defender and remains private to
the Attacker till the end of the game. At t ≥0, the Attacker
with type θ chooses its next position st+1 from its action
set:
Aθ(st) :=
(
N(st),
if st ̸= θ,
{θ},
otherwise,
i.e., if the Attacker reaches its selected goal, it stays there.
At each time step t ≥0, the Defender selects a single
goal aD
t
∈Θ to allocate resources to. Choosing action
aD
t = i corresponds to allocating w(st, st+1) units of defense
resource to goal i ∈Θ, where (st, st+1) is the edge traversed
by the Attacker at t. Once the resource is allocated to a goal,
it cannot be reallocated to other goals later on.
Terminal condition: The game ends when the Attacker
reaches its selected goal θ. We denote T θ to be the terminal
time for the Attacker type θ. Note that T θ is a random
variable conditioned on the Attacker θ’s strategy:
T θ = min
t∈Z≥0{t|st = θ}.
Information structure: The graph G and the candidate
goal set Θ are common information and are omitted in the
information sets of both players.
We denote public history of the Attacker’s positions as
ht = (s0, s1, . . . , st) with the initial history h0 = (s0), and
the set of all possible state histories at time t as Ht. We say
that hm is a prefix of a history ht, denoted as hm ⊑ht, if
∃h′ such that ht = (hm, h′). That is, the first m actions of
ht coincide exactly with all the actions in hm.
We denote the Attacker’s information set at time t = −1
as IA
t=−1 = (s0), which informs the Attacker about its initial
position. For t ≥0, we consider simultaneous actions. The
Attacker’s information set is a tuple IA
t
= (θ, ht), which
informs the Attacker about its selected type θ and its current
state history ht. The set of possible Attacker’s information
sets is denoted as IA
t , where IA
−1 = V and IA
t≥0 = Θ × Ht.
Since the Defender does not have access to θ, its information
set for t ≥0 is simply ID
t = (ht) ∈ID
t = Ht.
Strategy sets: We let Γ be the set of admissible behav-
ioral strategies of the Attacker, and γ = (γtype, (γθ)θ∈Θ) ∈Γ
to be a specific Attacker’s strategy. The Attacker’s selection
of a goal at t = −1 is given by a mapping γtype : IA
−1 →
∆(Θ), where ∆(Θ) denotes an |Θ|-dimensional simplex.
Policy γtype(θ|s0) determines the probability that the Attacker
selects goal θ given initial position s0. For the rest of the
game, i.e., for t ≥0, the Attacker type θ plays strategy
γθ, where γθ
ht : IA
t
→∆(Aθ(·)). The behavioral strategy
γθ
ht is a probability distribution over Aθ(st). Thus, γθ
ht(s′)
denotes the probability that the Attacker of type θ selects
next position s′ ∈Aθ(st) given history ht. Note that we
restrict Γ to be such that the Attacker type θ reaches its goal
in finite time, i.e., Pγ(T θ < ∞) = 1, ∀θ ∈Θ, and ∀γ ∈Γ.
Similarly, we use Π to denote the set of Defender’s
admissible behavioral strategies, and we let π ∈Π to be
its element, which provides the Defender’s strategy πht :
ID
t →∆(Θ). The strategy πht(aD
t ) denotes the probability
that the Defender allocates to goal aD
t ∈Θ after observing
history ht.
Probabilities: We denote by pθ
ht ≜Pγ(θ|ht) the prob-
ability that the Attacker is type θ given observed history ht.
This probability is conditioned on the Attacker’s strategy γ.
Since the Attacker chooses its type at t = −1, the elements
of the initial probability ph0 ∈∆(Θ) is given by pθ
h0 =
γtype(θ|s0). For t ≥0 the probability pht is propagated via
Bayes rule:
pθ
ht+1 =
pθ
htγθ
ht(st+1)
P
ψ∈Θ
pψ
htγψ
ht(st+1)
,
(1)
where ht+1 = (ht, st+1).
Stage cost: The goals have initial allocation described
by the vector r ∈R|Θ|
≥0. We let rθ := [r]θ denote the
component corresponding to goal θ. The initial allocation can
be viewed as the preparedness level assigned to each goal
for countering a potential attack. The Attacker’s stage cost
at t depends on the traversed edge (st, st+1), the Attacker’s


--- Page 3 ---
type θ and the Defender’s action aD
t :
lθ(st, st+1, aD
t ) =
(
w(st, st+1),
if aD
t = θ,
0,
otherwise.
(2)
In other words, the Attacker θ is penalized only when the
Defender allocates to the true goal aD
t = θ.
Objective function:
We consider a zero-sum game
where the Attacker aims to minimize the expected cumulative
cost, while the Defender aims to maximize it. Once the
Attacker chooses its goal θ, we can express the Attacker
θ’s total expected cost as follows:
Jθ(π, γθ; s0, r) = Eγθ,π


T θ−1
X
t=0
lθ(st, st+1, aD
t )

+ rθ.
Then the overall expected cost of the Attacker is:
J(π, γ; s0, r) = Eγ,π 
Jθ(π, γθ; s0, r)

=
X
θ∈Θ
γtype(θ|s0)Jθ(π, γθ; s0, r).
(3)
Note that the Attacker can employ a type-specific strategy
for each realized type, whereas the Defender must deploy
the same strategy across all possible Attacker types.
We consider an equilibrium concept defined as follows:
Definition 1: A strategy profile (γ∗, π∗) constitutes a
Nash equilibrium (NE) if for all γ ∈Γ and π ∈Π it satisfies:
J(π, γ∗; s0, r) ≤J(π∗, γ∗; s0, r) ≤J(π∗, γ; s0, r).
(4)
The next section describes the proposed solution method.
III. SOLUTION METHOD
The DPP game presented above is a zero-sum stochastic
game with asymmetric information in which the state is
controlled only by the informed agent, the Attacker. This
setting allows the use of the LP method presented in [15].
However, a key difference is that while [15] considers games
with known and fixed terminal time, our DPP game has free
terminal time: i.e., the game termination depends on the
Attacker’s strategy. Therefore, in Section III-A, we firstly
provide an LP method to solve a restricted game, which is a
prefix-closed truncation of the original extensive-form game
to a finite set of histories. In Section III-B, we secondly
provide a Double-Oracle algorithm inspired by [16], [17] that
iteratively expands the restricted game to find the solution for
the overall unrestricted game.
A. Solution for a restricted game.
We first analyze a restricted game, which is a prefix-closed
truncation of the original extensive-form game to a set of
histories ˆH ⊂S∞
t=0 Ht. By definition of prefix-closedness,
if a history ht+1 belongs to ˆH, then its prefix ht also belongs
to ˆH. We obtain the initial restricted game by propagating
the game tree up to a fixed time horizon τ > 0. Accordingly,
we define the initial set of histories ˆH as the set of histories
reachable by time τ, i.e., ˆH := Sτ
t=0 Ht.
We let H
θ := {ht ∈
ˆH|sm ̸= θ,
∀hm ⊑ht} to
be a set of non-terminal histories for Attacker θ in the
unrestricted game. Let Hθ
f ⊂
ˆH denote the set of final
histories for Attacker θ in the restricted game, defined as
the histories that either (i) reach the terminal condition (i.e.,
the corresponding goal) in the original game, or (ii) those
that lie on the truncation frontier t = τ. Based on the
above two cases, for each type θ, we partition Hθ
f into the
subset that ends the restricted game at the selected goal θ,
i.e., Hθ∗
f
:= ˆH \ H
θ, and its complement that ends at any
other state, i.e., H
θ
f := Hθ
f \ Hθ∗
f . For the latter, the original
game continues beyond τ. Any history ht ∈H
θ
f is treated
as terminal with an exogenously specified continuation cost
for Attacker θ denoted as Uθ
ht. The method for computing
this continuation cost is described in Section III-B.
For all non-terminal histories ht ∈H
θ, we can represent
the stage game as a matrix Lθ
ht ∈R|Θ|×|Aθ(st)|:

Lθ
ht

aD
t ,st+1 =





lθ(st, st+1, aD
t ) + Uθ
ht+1, if ht ∈H
θ
f,
lθ(st, st+1, aD
t ) + rθ, if ht = h0,
lθ(st, st+1, aD
t ), otherwise,
(5)
where aD
t ∈Θ is the row player’s (Defender’s) action, and
st+1 ∈Aθ(st) is the column player’s (Attacker’s) action. For
any terminal history that ends the overall unrestricted game
for Attacker θ, i.e., ht ∈Hθ∗
f , we have Lθ
ht = 0|Θ|×|Aθ(st)|.
We denote the information sets of the Attacker (resp. the
Defender) in the restricted game for t = −1 as ˆIA
−1 = V
and for t ≥0 as ˆIA
t = Ht × Θ, (resp. for t ≥0 as ˆID
t
=
Ht), where Ht ∈ˆH. We let ˆΓ be the Attacker’s admissible
strategy set in the restricted game, and its element be an
Attacker’s strategy ˆγ := (ˆγtype, (ˆγθ)θ∈Θ). Here ˆγtype : ˆIA
−1 →
∆(Θ), and ˆγθ
ht : ˆIA
t →∆(Aθ(·)). Similarly we define the
Defender’s strategy set in the restricted game as ˆΠ and its
element ˆπ, where ˆπht : ˆID
t →∆(Θ).
The quantity J ∗
ht(pht, r) represents the equilibrium ex-
pected cost from history ht onward. We define the equi-
librium expected continuation cost starting from history ht
given the probability pht over the Attacker types as the
following min-max problem:
J ∗
ht(pht, r) = max
ˆπ
min
ˆγ
X
θ∈Θ
pθ
htJ θ
ht(ˆπ, ˆγθ, r),
(6)
where the probability pht is defined by (1), ph0 = γtype.
In (6) term J θ
ht(ˆπ, ˆγθ, r) is the expected cost for the Attacker
type θ at history ht, when the strategies in the restricted game
are ˆπ and ˆγθ:
J θ
ht(ˆπ, ˆγθ, r) = ˆπ⊤
htLθ
htˆγθ
ht+
(7a)
X
st+1∈Aθ(st)
ˆγθ
ht(st+1)J θ
ht+1(ˆπ, ˆγθ, r), ∀ht ∈ˆH \ Hθ
f,
J θ
ht(ˆπ, ˆγθ, r) = ˆπ⊤
htLθ
htˆγθ
ht, ∀ht ∈Hθ
f.
(7b)
By directly applying the results of [15], the Attacker’s
strategy for a restricted game can be found via the following


--- Page 4 ---
primal LP:
min
ˆγtype, (zθ
ht)θ∈Θ,
vht,∀ht∈ˆ
H
X
ht∈ˆ
H
vht
(8)
s.t. 1⊤zθ
h0 = ˆγtype(θ|s0),
1⊤ˆγtype = 1, ˆγtype ≥0,
and ∀ht+1 = (ht, st+1), ht, ht+1 ∈ˆH :
X
θ∈Θ
Lθ
htzθ
ht ≤vht1,
1⊤zθ
ht+1 = zθ
ht(st+1), zθ
ht ≥0
Here zθ
ht = pθ
htˆγθ
ht. The solution for the Attacker’s strat-
egy in the restricted game is given by the optimal min-
imizing arguments ˆγ∗
type and zθ∗
ht of (8). Note that ˆγ∗=
(ˆγ∗
type, (ˆγθ∗)θ∈Θ), where ˆγθ∗
ht for ht ∈ˆH is given by:
ˆγθ∗
ht (s′) =





zθ∗
ht (s′)
P
ˆs∈Aθ(st)
zθ∗
ht (ˆs),
if
P
ˆs∈Aθ(st)
zθ∗
ht (ˆs) > 0,
1
|Aθ(st)|,
otherwise.
Defender’s optimal strategy ˆπ∗is given by ˆπ∗
ht in solution
of the following dual LP:
max
ˆπht,(qθ
ht)θ∈Θ,
U, ∀ht∈ˆ
H
U
(9)
s.t. ∀θ ∈Θ, ht ⊑ht+1, ht, ht+1 ∈ˆH :
1⊤ˆπht = 1, ˆπht ≥0, and qθ
h0 ≥U,
Lθ⊤
ht ˆπht +
h
qθ
ht+1
i
st+1∈Aθ(st) ≥qθ
ht1, ∀ht ∈ˆH \ Hθ
f,
Lθ⊤
ht πht ≥qθ
ht1,
∀ht ∈Hθ
f.
Note that it is unnecessary to solve the LP (9) to find the
solution for the Defender’s strategy ˆπ∗, one can extract the
Defender’s strategy from the dual variables for the optimality
constraints of the primal LP (8).
In the next subsection we lay out an algorithm that utilizes
the LP in (8) and iteratively expands the restricted game.
B. Default strategies.
Default strategies specify the actions outside the restricted
game in order to specify the continuation values Uθ
ht+1 at
the leaf nodes, i.e. nodes corresponding to histories ht ∈H
θ
f,
(see (5)). There are two key considerations for our selection
of the default strategies. Firstly, we ensure that Uθ
ht are
optimistic estimates of the true values from the Attacker’s
perspective, which is necessary for the optimality result pre-
sented in Proposition 1. To this end, we select the Attacker’s
default strategy to be a best response to the Defender’s
default strategy. Secondly, we select the Defender’s strategy
so that the aforementioned best response can be computed
easily.
Defining the default strategies explicitly might take a large
amount of memory, but in practice they can be defined using
some general rule. We set the Defender’s default strategy,
denoted as ˜π ∈Π, as allocation to the goal closest to the
Attacker:
˜πht(θclose) = 1, where θclose = arg min
ψ∈Θ d(st, ψ).
(10)
In case of a tie, the Defender selects the goal with the
smallest index.
The Attacker’s default strategy is found as a deterministic
best response to ˜π. Since we selected ˜π to depend only on
the Attacker’s current location, the Attacker’s best response
can be formulated as a shortest path problem on an MDP.
The optimal strategy ˜γθ
ht and the value V θ∗(s), ∀s ∈V of
this shortest path problem can be found via standard value
iteration and the Bellman equation [18].1 This optimal value
provides the continuation cost in the restricted game, Uθ
ht =
V θ∗(st).
C. Expansion of the restricted game.
Algorithm 1 summarizes the process to expand the restricted
game based on the XDO algorithm. Starting from the initial
ˆH, we compute the Attacker’s strategy ˆγ∗for the restricted
game by solving LP (8). For each Attacker type θ played
with positive probability, i.e., ˆγ∗
type(θ|s0) > 0, we identify
the reachable frontier histories
H
θ,ˆγ∗
f
:= {ht ∈H
θ
f | Pˆγ∗(ht|θ) ˆγ∗
type(θ|s0) > 0}.
(11)
Each history ht ∈S
θ∈Θ H
θ,ˆγ∗
f
is expanded by one time step.
The resulting histories are added to ˆH, and the restricted
game is solved again with the updated stage-cost matrices
Lθ
ht. This process iteratively enlarges the restricted game
and terminates when the optimal Attacker strategy no-longer
leads to a non-terminal history, i.e., S
θ∈Θ H
θ,ˆγ∗
f
= {∅}.
Algorithm 1 Solve unrestricted Game
Require: initial ˆH,
ˆγ∗for initial ˆH given by LP (8)
1: For all θ ∈Θ find H
θ,ˆγ∗
f
using (11)
2: while S
θ∈Θ H
θ,ˆγ∗
f
̸= {∅} do
% Non-terminal history exists
3:
for ht ∈S
θ∈Θ H
θ,ˆγ∗
f
do
4:
for st+1 ∈N(st) do
5:
ht+1 = (ht, st+1)
% Create a new history
6:
ˆH ←ˆH ∪{ht+1}
7:
end for
8:
end for
9:
For every type θ ∈Θ reassign Lθ
ht for every ht ∈ˆH
10:
Solve for ˆγ∗using LP (8)
11:
For all θ ∈Θ find new H
θ,ˆγ∗
f
using (11)
12: end while
13: Solve for ˆπ∗using LP (9)
14: return ˆπ∗, ˆγ∗, ˆH
Algorithm 1 is conceptually similar to the A∗search algo-
rithm [19]. The continuation cost Uθ
ht acts as an optimistic
1To ensure that the Attacker reaches the goal in finite time, an arbitrarily
small number can be added to the stage cost.


--- Page 5 ---
heuristic estimate of the remaining cost for the Attacker.
It never overestimates the true equilibrium expected cost
because Uθ
ht is computed as the Attacker’s best response
to the Defender’s default strategy. Therefore, histories that
are not reached with positive probability under the current
optimal strategy ˆγ∗cannot reduce the Attacker’s equilibrium
cost and can be safely ignored.
The equilibrium Attacker’s strategy is given by ˆγ∗, ob-
tained from Algorithm 1, i.e., γ∗= ˆγ∗. The Defender’s
equilibrium strategy is given by ˆπ∗from Algorithm 1 and
the default strategy ˜π:
π∗
ht(θ) =
(
ˆπ∗
ht(θ), if ht ∈ˆH,
˜πht(θ), otherwise.
(12)
Proposition 1: The output of Algorithm 1 (π∗, γ∗) con-
stitutes a Nash equilibrium of the original unrestricted game:
i.e., it satisfies (4).
Proof.
The linear programs (8) and (9) produce strategies
for both players that constitute a Nash equilibrium in the
restricted game by construction.
Moreover, for any Attacker strategy ˆγ such that Pˆγ(ht) =
0 for all ht ∈H
θ
f, the cost in the restricted game coincides
with the cost in the full game for any Defender policy. Since
the Attacker alone controls the evolution of the game history,
the probability Pˆγ(ht) is independent of the Defender’s
strategy. Consequently, the Defender cannot force the game
to reach any history ht ∈H
θ
f, and thus the Defender’s best
response in the restricted game is also a best response in the
full game.
It therefore remains to show that the Attacker has no
incentive to use a strategy that generates histories ht ∈
H
θ
f. This property holds once Algorithm 1 converges. The
algorithm terminates when the while loop exits, which occurs
when every selected Attacker type reaches its intended goal
in the restricted game, i.e., when Pˆγ(ht) = 0 for all ht ∈H
θ
f.
Under this condition, any deviation by the Attacker that
drives the game to a history ht ∈H
θ
f incurs a cost that
is at least the continuation cost computed for that history.
This continuation cost is evaluated assuming a best response
by the Attacker to the Defender’s default strategy. Since the
Defender indeed uses the default strategy for such histories
under π∗, the Attacker cannot obtain a lower cost in the full
game by deviating in this manner. Therefore, the Attacker
has no incentive to generate histories in H
θ
f, and (π∗, γ∗)
constitutes a Nash equilibrium of the full game.
□
Note that Algorithm 1 is guaranteed to terminate in finite
time if there exists an equilibrium strategy for Attacker
such that all histories generated by it reach the goal in
bounded time. Although there may be cases where Attacker’s
equilibrium strategy must generate histories which do not
reach the goal for an arbitrarily long time, this has not
occurred in any tested examples, such as those in Section V,
and we conjecture that this does not occur.
D. Discussion
In equilibrium, the Attacker may assign zero probability to
some types θ, i.e., γ∗
type(θ|s0) = 0, because the corresponding
goal may not be worth pursuing. Suppose the equilibrium
Attacker selects multiple types θ, ψ ∈Θ with positive
probability, i.e., γ∗
type(θ|s0) > 0 and γ∗
type(ψ|s0) > 0. Then
the equilibrium expected costs for these types must be
equal. Otherwise, if Jθ(π∗, γθ∗; s0, r) < Jψ(π∗, γψ∗; s0, r),
the Attacker could strictly decrease its expected cost by
assigning higher probability to type θ and zero probability
to type ψ, contradicting the optimality of γ∗.
A
similar
argument
applies
to
trajectories.
Given
γ∗
type(θ|s0) > 0, all terminal histories ht ∈Hθ∗
f
realized
by γθ∗must yield the same equilibrium expected cost.
Otherwise, the Attacker could reduce its cost by shifting
probability to a trajectory with a lower cost. Thus, the
equilibrium Defender makes the Attacker indifferent between
its equilibrium type(s) and equilibrium trajectories.
Remark 1 (On-path indifference): Let ˆΘ := {θ ∈Θ |
γ∗
type(θ|s0) > 0}. Then:
(i) Indifference between equilibrium types: For any θ, ψ ∈
ˆΘ,
Jθ(π∗, γθ∗; s0, r) = Jψ(π∗, γψ∗; s0, r).
(ii) Indifference between equilibrium trajectories: For any
θ ∈ˆΘ, all terminal histories ht ∈Hθ∗
f
that occur
with positive probability under γθ∗yield the same
equilibrium expected cost.
In the next section we propose two metrics to quantify
deceptive behavior of the Attacker.
IV. DECEPTION METRICS
To evaluate deceptive behavior in the incomplete information
game, we first establish the benchmark value of the complete
information game, when the Defender knows the Attacker’s
selected goal θ at the start of the game. This benchmark
allows us to define two metrics: a) Risk of Deception (RoD) –
the expected penalty incurred by the Attacker when a decep-
tive strategy is used against a fully informed Defender; and
b) Value of Information (VoI) [20] – the relative advantage
the Attacker gains from private information compared to the
complete information case. Together, these metrics capture
the trade-off between risk and benefit in deceptive play.
Complete Information Game: Suppose the Defender
knows the Attacker’s type θ at the start of the game. In this
case, the Defender’s optimal strategy is to allocate to θ every
time step, while the Attacker’s best response is to follow
the shortest path to its goal. The cost to the Attacker θ is
therefore the sum of the initial allocation and the distance to
θ (length of the shortest path to θ). If the Attacker anticipates
that its type will be revealed after selection, it will always
choose the type minimizing this cost.
Remark 2 (Complete Information Game Value): The
value of the complete information game for type θ is
¯U θ∗(s0, rθ) = d(s0, θ) + rθ,
(13)


--- Page 6 ---
and the overall value is
¯U ∗(s0, r) = min
θ∈Θ
¯U θ∗(s0, rθ).
(14)
This benchmark provides a natural reference point for
defining both RoD and VoI.
Risk of Deception (RoD): In complete information
game, the Attacker’s deceptive strategy that deviates from
a shortest path will be suboptimal. Consequently, when the
Attacker is unsure whether the Defender does or does not
know its private type, the Attacker faces a dilemma: (i)
attempt at deceiving the Defender; or (ii) remain on the
shortest path. If the Defender knows θ, then the cost of
playing γθ is the expected path length, i.e.:
ˆdγθ(s0, rθ) := Eγθ


T θ−1
X
t=0
w(st, st+1) + rθ

.
Definition 2: The risk of deception for Attacker θ is the
excess cost of playing γθ∗instead of staying on a shortest
path when the Defender knows θ:
RoDθ(s0, r) = ˆdγθ∗(s0, rθ) −¯U θ∗(s0, r).
The expected risk of the overall strategy γ∗is
RoD(s0, r) =
X
θ∈Θ
γ∗
type(θ|s0) RoDθ(s0, r).
If RoDθ(s0, r) = 0, it implies that Attacker type θ always
remains on a shortest path. Likewise, RoD(s0, r) = 0 implies
that all types selected with non-zero probability stay on
shortest paths. Positive RoD values quantify the expected
penalty that the Attacker faces when attempting deception
against an informed Defender.
Value of Information (VoI): VoI [20] quantifies the
(normalized) advantage that the Attacker gains from having
a private information, relative to the complete information
benchmark.
Definition 3: For Attacker θ, the value of information is
VoIθ(s0, r) =
¯U θ∗(s0, r) −Jθ(π∗, γθ∗; s0, r)
¯U θ∗(s0, r)
.
The overall value of information is
VoI(s0, r) =
¯U ∗(s0, r) −J(π∗, γ∗; s0, r)
¯U ∗(s0, r)
.
A positive value of information VoI(s0, r) > 0 indicates
that private information will provide a positive benefit to
the Attacker. Note that ¯U θ∗(s0, r) = 0 occurs only in the
degenerate case where the Attacker starts at goal θ with zero
initial allocation, i.e., rθ = 0, and the game immediately
ends. Thus we assume that the denominator is nonzero.
Otherwise, we define VoI(s0, r) = 0.
The two measures are complementary: RoD highlights the
vulnerability of deceptive strategies when they are revealed,
while VoI highlights the potential benefit of deception when
the Attacker’s type remains private.
g1
g2
g3
Start
g1
g2
g3
Start
g1
g2
g3
Start
g4
g1
g2
g3
Start
g4
RoD = 0
</latexit>a) VoI = 0.37
4XgbtcMgpipIFQyfWumPaJBR0XQVdgjP/5UWon1hO2Srfn5UqN0/TOvJoHx2gI+SgC1RBt6iKaoiIXpGr+jNyIwX4934mI7mjFmFe+iPjM8fV0+Tw=</latexit>b) VoI = 0.35
RoD = 0
c) VoI = 0
RoD = 0
d) VoI = 0.54
RoD = 12.11
Fig. 2.
Equilibrium strategies for different initial conditions. Equilibrium
cost is: a) 61.54, b) 63.15, c) 48.52, d) 45.30.
V. NUMERICAL ILLUSTRATION
We illustrate the equilibrium solution in two settings: a realis-
tic road network and a grid world. We highlight how optimal
deception emerges and how it depends on environment struc-
ture and initial conditions. Across both settings we contrast
outcomes under asymmetric and complete information by
showing the value of the game, VoI, and RoD.
A. Road network.
Figure 2 illustrates scenarios on a road-network graph ex-
tracted from real map data and presents VoI := VoI(s0, r)
and RoD := RoD(s0, r). Across all cases we set the initial
allocations to zero, i.e., rθ = 0, ∀θ ∈Θ.
In Fig. 2a, the Attacker mixes uniformly over all goals
and, conditional on the chosen goal, follows a shortest path
toward it. In Fig. 2b goal g1 is relocated. The equilibrium
Attacker never pursues g1 and randomizes uniformly over
g2 and g3. Consequently, VoI decreases relative to Fig. 2a
because the path to g1 no longer aids concealment. Note that
RoD = 0 in both Fig. 2a and 2b, which means that the paths
taken by the equilibrium Attacker are the shortest ones, yet
VoI > 0. This highlights the benefit the equilibrium Attacker
receives from the Defender’s informational disadvantage.
Figures 2c and 2d add a new goal g4. On Fig. 2c the
Attacker selects only g4 and follows the shortest path. Here
VoI = 0, yet the Attacker’s total cost is lower than in Fig. 2a,
illustrating that VoI does not directly track the equilibrium
payoff. In Fig. 2d, the placement of g4 induces uniform
mixing over all four goals, with routes to g1 and g4 transiting
through g3. Here the Attacker chooses a non-shortest path
to g1, yielding RoD > 0, while VoI has the highest value
among the four cases.
B. Grid world.
Figure 3 presents a grid-world scenario with three goals. All
values depict equilibrium performance under rθ = 0, ∀θ ∈
Θ. The bottom-right subfigure shows that when the Attacker
starts close to a particular goal, it often commits to that goal


--- Page 7 ---
g1
/KBZEdeLo=">AB6nicbVBNSwMxEJ2tX7V+VT16CRbBU9ktpXosePFY0X5Au5Zsmt2GZpMlyQpl6U

4EixkBsr3UePtWG54lbdBdA68XJSgRytYflrMJIkjakwhGOt+56bGD/DyjDC6aw0SDVNMJngiPYtFTim2s8Wp87QhVGKJTKljBof6eyHCs9TQObGeMzVivenPxP6+fmvDaz5hIUkMFWS4KU46MRPO/0Yg

RoD
g3

g3
g1
Selected goals
{g2}
{g3}
{g2, g3}
{g1}
{g1, g2, g3}
Fig. 3.
Equilibrium values for expected cost (top left), VoI (top right),
RoD (bottom left), and set of selected goals, i.e. γ∗
type(θ|s0) > 0 (bottom
right).
alone. The game then becomes equivalent to a complete-
information case, which is reflected by VoI being zero, as
shown in the top-right subfigure. In contrast, the dark-green
region (labeled as {g1, g2, g3}) away from all three goals
lead to mixing between all three goals. Concealing the intent
provides a tangible advantage captured by positive VoI,
and the Attacker often accepts non-shortest path detours, as
evidenced by positive RoD at certain initial positions.
VI. CONCLUSION
We formulated a new variant of the deceptive path plan-
ning (DPP) game with goal selection, and introduced a
solution method that integrates linear programming with the
Double-Oracle algorithm. We propose a particular choice of
default strategies that allow us to solve DPP game which
has indefinite horizon, and it also ensures our solution is an
exact Nash equilibrium, rather than an ε-Nash. We present
two complementary metrics to quantify the benefits and
costs of deception: Value of Information (VoI) and Risk
of Deception (RoD). Together, these measures delineate
conditions where deception is both effective and risk-averse.
The illustrative examples reveal how deceptive behavior can
improve performance. Future work will address scenarios
with partial observability, stochastic dynamics and games in
continuous space.
REFERENCES
[1] M. Lloyd, The Art of Military Deception.
Pen and Sword, 2003.
[2] M. H. Almeshekah and E. H. Spafford, “Cyber security deception,”
Cyber Deception: Building the Scientific Foundation, pp. 23–50, 2016.
[3] A. Nikitas, S. Parkinson, and M. Vallati, “The deceitful connected
and autonomous vehicle: Defining the concept, contextualising its
dimensions and proposing mitigation policies,” Transport policy, vol.
122, pp. 1–10, 2022.
[4] C. Wayllace, P. Hou, W. G. S. Yeoh, and T. C. Son, “Goal recognition
design with stochastic agent action outcomes,” in Int. Joint Conf. on
Artif. Intell., 2016, pp. 3279–3285.
[5] R. Pereira, N. Oren, and F. Meneguzzi, “Landmark-based heuristics
for goal recognition,” in AAAI Conf. Artif. Intell., vol. 31, no. 1, 2017.
[6] M. Ramırez and H. Geffner, “Goal recognition over POMDPs: Infer-
ring the intention of a POMDP agent,” in Int. Joint Conf. on Artif.
Intell., 2011, pp. 2009–2014.
[7] A. Dragan, R. Holladay, and S. Srinivasa, “Deceptive robot motion:
synthesis, analysis and experiments,” Autonomous Robots, vol. 39, pp.
331–345, 2015.
[8] P. Masters and S. Sardina, “Deceptive path-planning,” in Int. Joint
Conf. on Artif. Intell., 2017, pp. 4368–4375.
[9] M. Ornik and U. Topcu, “Deception in optimal control,” in 2018 56th
Annu. Allerton Conf. on Communication, Control, and Comput., pp.
821–828.
[10] M. O. Karabag, M. Ornik, and U. Topcu, “Deception in supervisory
control,” IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control, vol. 67, no. 2, pp.
738–753, 2021.
[11] V. Rostobaya, Y. Guan, J. Berneburg, M. Dorothy, and D. Shishika,
“Deception by motion: The eater and the mover game,” IEEE Control
Systems Letters, vol. 7, pp. 3157–3162, 2023.
[12] V. Rostobaya, J. Berneburg, Y. Guan, M. Dorothy, and D. Shishika,
“Deceptive path planning: A bayesian game approach,” arXiv preprint
arXiv:2506.13650, 2025.
[13] D. Koller, N. Megiddo, and B. Von Stengel, “Efficient computation
of equilibria for extensive two-person games,” Games and economic
behavior, vol. 14, no. 2, pp. 247–259, 1996.
[14] Y. Guan, D. Maity, and P. Tsiotras, “Strategic concealment of envi-
ronment representations in competitive games,” IEEE Control Systems
Letters, vol. 9, pp. 2609–2614, 2025.
[15] L. Li and J. Shamma, “LP formulation of asymmetric zero-sum
stochastic games,” in 53rd IEEE Conf. on Decision and Control, 2014,
pp. 1930–1935.
[16] S. McAleer, J. B. Lanier, K. A. Wang, P. Baldi, and R. Fox, “XDO:
A double oracle algorithm for extensive-form games,” Advances in
Neural Information Processing Systems, vol. 34, pp. 23 128–23 139,
2021.
[17] B. Bosansky, C. Kiekintveld, V. Lisy, and M. Pechoucek, “An exact
double-oracle algorithm for zero-sum extensive-form games with
imperfect information,” J. Artif. Intell. Res., vol. 51, pp. 829–866,
2014.

Springer Science & Business Media, 2012.
[19] P. E. Hart, N. J. Nilsson, and B. Raphael, “A formal basis for the
heuristic determination of minimum cost paths,” IEEE transactions on
Systems Science and Cybernetics, vol. 4, no. 2, pp. 100–107, 1968.
[20] R. A. Howard, “Information value theory,” IEEE Transactions on
Systems Science and Cybernetics, vol. 2, no. 1, pp. 22–26, 1966.
